Is Grad School Worth It for CS Majors?

What to Notice

This view is designed as a compact proof machine for one claim: a Master’s degree can produce a long-run financial advantage, but only when debt, salary growth, and repayment assumptions remain favorable. The break-even chart shows when cumulative net earnings from the Master path catch and exceed the Bachelor path. That crossing point is sensitive: higher debt or interest can shift it years later, and in some scenarios the crossing never happens inside the 30-year window. The annual advantage chart adds another layer by showing how much the Master path leads or lags in each year after graduation. Use the sliders to test realistic personal conditions—not best-case assumptions. For example, increasing taxes and cost-of-living often reduces purchasing-power gains even when headline salary looks strong. The goal is not to force one answer, but to reveal the decision boundary where graduate school stops being a clear financial win and becomes a tradeoff requiring non-financial justification.

Annotation: Waiting for model update...

Beyond the Numbers

Set your preferences to receive a personalized non-financial recommendation.

💡 Tip: This calculator shows raw financial outcomes. Consider non-financial factors like career fulfillment, job market demand, and personal goals when making your decision.

Model Assumptions

  • Loan Repayment: Standard amortization schedule over the specified period
  • Monthly Payment: Fixed payment calculated from principal, rate, and term
  • Salary Growth: Constant annual percentage increase for both paths after graduation
  • Master's Salary Premium: Master graduates start at a higher salary immediately after graduation
  • Debt Impact: Total debt is deducted from cumulative earnings over time
  • Break-even: First year where Master's cumulative net earnings exceed Bachelor's
  • No Career Gaps: Assumes continuous employment and salary growth
  • Taxes: Uses your estimated tax rate as a simple net-income approximation
  • Cost of Living: Uses a location cost index multiplier to normalize purchasing power
  • ROI Method: Net earnings = (Salary − Taxes) / Cost Index − Debt Payment

What This Data Does Not Show

  • Job satisfaction and career fulfillment
  • Burnout and work-life balance
  • Employer-paid tuition and education assistance
  • Promotions and stock compensation
  • Regional variation in salaries and cost of living
  • Non-software engineering CS careers
